cluster_name: Nigel Farage triggers Clacton by-election after resigning amid financial probe
tldr: Headlines split sharply by geography: US right-wing and Russian outlets lead with Farage's anti-establishment gamble framing while UK, Canadian, pan-Arab, and Brazilian outlets foreground the financial scandal. Readers scanning only homepage titles will encounter either a political insurgent seizing democratic legitimacy or a politician resigning under a financial cloud — a wider divergence than body text sustains.
event_summary: Reform UK leader Nigel Farage resigned as MP for Clacton on 7 July 2026, triggering a by-election he intends to contest himself, while under parliamentary investigation into a £5 million gift from a crypto billionaire and alleged undeclared benefits from a convicted fraudster.

evidence.quote: UK: Farage gambles on by-election to silence critics
Reform UK Accuses Establishment of 'Running Scared'
Nigel Farage steps down to force election
Reform activists urged to switch focus from Manchester
Farage to resign as MP amid finance scrutiny
após acusações sobre suas finanças pessoais
Farage resigns as MP amid finances scandal
voters blast Reform leader's 'weird' by-election plot

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Standard Kenya World reported UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer's resignation — a separate UK political story unrelated to the Farage by-election.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to headline Farage's frame from the establishment's perspective — accusing rivals of 'running scared' — with no hedging qualifier, making the populist attack the news rather than the by-election itself.

Only headline to foreground voter reaction over electoral strategy, using 'voters blast' and the scare-quoted 'weird' — making PUBLIC_OPINION the opening register rather than POLITICAL strategy or financial scandal.
Headlines a different resignation entirely — UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer — making it the only corpus item whose title concerns neither Farage nor the Clacton by-election.
Only outlet to label Farage 'hard-right' in its headline — the most pointed ideological descriptor in the corpus; all other outlets use 'Reform UK leader' or no political label.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, US right-wing and Russian outlets amplify Farage's anti-establishment gamble narrative wholesale while UK, Canadian, Brazilian, and pan-Arab outlets lead with the financial scandal — a geographic frame split sharper at the homepage level than in body text, with Australia's headline uniquely reducing the entire story to the spectacle of a comedian in a rubbish bin.
